source_paper_title: Robust Sparsification for Matroid Intersection with Applications

Page 6, Introduction (end of the discussion on density-based decomposition and principal partitions).

Moreover, in our algorithms, as we frequently update the ground set on which we compute the decomposition by adding or removing one element, there may be room to improve our time complexity: we leave as an open question whether updating a density-based decomposition when performing these kinds of operations can be done more efficiently, without re-computing the whole decomposition each time.
title: Efficiently update density-based decompositions under single-element insertions and deletions
background_latex:
Let $M=(V,\mathcal I)$ be a matroid with rank function $\operatorname{rank}_M:2^V\to\mathbb Z_{\ge 0}$. For any nonempty $U\subseteq V$ with $\operatorname{rank}_M(U)>0$, define its density by
\[\rho_M(U)=\frac{|U|}{\operatorname{rank}_M(U)}.\]
(With the conventions: $\rho_M(\emptyset)=0$ and $\rho_M(U)=+\infty$ if $U\neq\emptyset$ and $\operatorname{rank}_M(U)=0$.)

For a subset $V'\subseteq V$, let $M|_{V'}$ denote the restriction of $M$ to $V'$. Consider the greedy procedure that, starting from $M|_{V'}$, iteratively selects a densest subset of maximum cardinality and then contracts it, producing a partition (allowing empty parts)
\[V'=U_1\cup U_2\cup\cdots\cup U_k,\]
where $k=\operatorname{rank}_M(V)$ and $U_j$ is a maximum-cardinality densest subset in the contracted matroid $(M|_{V'})/(U_1\cup\cdots\cup U_{j-1})$.
This partition is called the density-based decomposition of $V'$ in $M$.

Suppose we maintain such a decomposition while $V'$ is dynamically updated by single-element operations: insertion of an element $u\in V\setminus V'$ (replacing $V'$ by $V'\cup\{u\}$) or deletion of an element $u\in V'$ (replacing $V'$ by $V'\setminus\{u\}$). A naive update recomputes the entire density-based decomposition from scratch after each operation, using (in general) submodular function minimization to find densest sets.
problem_definition_latex: Design an algorithm that maintains the density-based decomposition $U_1,\dots,U_k$ of $V'$ in a matroid $M$ under a sequence of single-element insertions and deletions to $V'$, and does so more efficiently than recomputing the whole decomposition from scratch after every update.
latex:
Let $M=(V,\mathcal I)$ be a matroid with rank function $\operatorname{rank}_M:2^V\to\mathbb Z_{\ge 0}$. For any nonempty $U\subseteq V$ with $\operatorname{rank}_M(U)>0$, define its density by
\[\rho_M(U)=\frac{|U|}{\operatorname{rank}_M(U)}.\]
(With the conventions: $\rho_M(\emptyset)=0$ and $\rho_M(U)=+\infty$ if $U\neq\emptyset$ and $\operatorname{rank}_M(U)=0$.)

For a subset $V'\subseteq V$, let $M|_{V'}$ denote the restriction of $M$ to $V'$. Consider the greedy procedure that, starting from $M|_{V'}$, iteratively selects a densest subset of maximum cardinality and then contracts it, producing a partition (allowing empty parts)
\[V'=U_1\cup U_2\cup\cdots\cup U_k,\]
where $k=\operatorname{rank}_M(V)$ and $U_j$ is a maximum-cardinality densest subset in the contracted matroid $(M|_{V'})/(U_1\cup\cdots\cup U_{j-1})$.
This partition is called the density-based decomposition of $V'$ in $M$.

Suppose we maintain such a decomposition while $V'$ is dynamically updated by single-element operations: insertion of an element $u\in V\setminus V'$ (replacing $V'$ by $V'\cup\{u\}$) or deletion of an element $u\in V'$ (replacing $V'$ by $V'\setminus\{u\}$). A naive update recomputes the entire density-based decomposition from scratch after each operation, using (in general) submodular function minimization to find densest sets.

Design an algorithm that maintains the density-based decomposition $U_1,\dots,U_k$ of $V'$ in a matroid $M$ under a sequence of single-element insertions and deletions to $V'$, and does so more efficiently than recomputing the whole decomposition from scratch after every update.
location_in_paper: Page 6, Introduction (end of the discussion on density-based decomposition and principal partitions).
open_problem_quote: Moreover, in our algorithms, as we frequently update the ground set on which we compute the decomposition by adding or removing one element, there may be room to improve our time complexity: we leave as an open question whether updating a density-based decomposition when performing these kinds of operations can be done more efficiently, without re-computing the whole decomposition each time.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: This is an algorithm-design and data-structure question rather than a universal inequality that admits finite counterexamples. Automated search over small matroids may suggest ideas, but failure on instances would not constitute a definitive counterexample to the existence of a faster update algorithm.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can represent candidate update procedures/data structures as code and score them by runtime (or oracle-call counts) and correctness on randomly generated matroids with independence or rank oracles. However, generating representative matroid oracles and proving asymptotic guarantees is difficult, so automated evolution is more likely to yield heuristics or experimentally faster methods than provably efficient algorithms.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The density-based decomposition in Huang–Sellier is a greedy rephrasing of the principal sequence/principal partition of a matroid restriction \(M|_{V'}\): it repeatedly selects a maximum-cardinality densest set \(U_j\) in the current contraction and contracts it, yielding parts \(U_1,\dots,U_k\) with strictly decreasing densities. The paper’s key structural contribution toward dynamic maintenance is the pair of modification lemmas (Lemmas 20–21), which show a strong Lipschitz/sensitivity phenomenon under single-element updates of \(V'\): insertion makes all associated densities \(\tilde\rho_M(v)\) monotone nondecreasing and confines changes to elements whose old density lies in an interval of length 1 around the inserted element’s old density (and symmetrically for deletions). These statements suggest that the decomposition changes “locally in density space,” a property reminiscent of stability results for principal partitions.

Subsequent/related work on submodular water-filling (e.g., in the online submodular assignment problem and online matroid intersection via submodular water-filling) studies essentially the same densest-set contraction chain for polymatroids/submodular functions, providing minimax and submodular-minimization characterizations of the level sets and proving monotonicity/locality properties under changes in weights. While these frameworks do not address fully dynamic insert/delete updates of the ground set \(V'\), they offer alternative viewpoints (unique maximal minimizers, market/convex duality) that could be leveraged to design incremental algorithms.

At present, none of the forward-citing analyses provided contains an explicit data structure or algorithm with provably improved update time for maintaining the full decomposition \(U_1,\dots,U_k\) under single-element insertions/deletions, beyond recomputation via repeated submodular minimization. Thus the problem remains open. Promising directions include exploiting Lemmas 20–21 to update only a small contiguous block of levels, and specializing to structured matroids (laminar, transversal) where densest-set computations admit faster primitives, potentially enabling polylogarithmic or near-linear-in-affected-region update times.

sources.title: Sparsification for Graph Matching and Matroid Intersection
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper introduces the density-based decomposition (a greedy/principal-sequence decomposition by iteratively taking a maximum-cardinality densest set and contracting) and proves two “modification lemmas” describing how the associated densities \,\(\tilde\rho_M(v)\) change under a single-element insertion/deletion in the maintained subset \(V'\):
- (Insertion, Lemma 20) For all \(v\in V\), \(\tilde\rho^{new}_M(v)\ge \tilde\rho^{old}_M(v)\); for the inserted element \(u\), \(\tilde\rho^{old}_M(u)\le \tilde\rho^{new}_M(u)\le \tilde\rho^{old}_M(u)+1\); and elements with \(\tilde\rho^{old}_M(v)\notin [\tilde\rho^{old}_M(u),\tilde\rho^{old}_M(u)+1]\) keep the same associated density.
- (Deletion, Lemma 21) Symmetric monotonicity: \(\tilde\rho^{new}_M(v)\le \tilde\rho^{old}_M(v)\); for the deleted element \(u\), \(\tilde\rho^{old}_M(u)\ge \tilde\rho^{new}_M(u)\ge \tilde\rho^{old}_M(u)-1\); and elements with \(\tilde\rho^{old}_M(v)\notin [\tilde\rho^{old}_M(u)-1,\tilde\rho^{old}_M(u)]\) keep the same associated density.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an algorithm that maintains the full density-based decomposition \(U_1,\dots,U_k\) of \(V'\) under single-element insertions/deletions faster than recomputation (which may require submodular minimization each time). This paper does not give such a dynamic maintenance algorithm or improved update-time bounds. However, it develops structural sensitivity results (Lemmas 20–21) precisely about how the decomposition’s induced associated densities change under one-element updates. These lemmas are a key prerequisite for designing efficient dynamic update algorithms (the authors explicitly note this as an open direction), and they are used to implement a local-search construction of a sparsifier (DCS) via repeated insert/delete operations.

sources.title: The online submodular assignment problem
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper develops a “water level” vector for polymatroids/submodular functions and gives an iterative densest-set contraction procedure (Algorithm 1) producing nested sets \(\emptyset=S_0\subset S_1\subset\cdots\subset S_L=E\) with densities \(t_1>\cdots>t_L\), assigning each element \(e\in S_{\ell}\setminus S_{\ell-1}\) a level \(w_e=t_{\ell}= rac{x(S_{\ell}\setminus S_{\ell-1})}{f_{S_{\ell-1}}(S_{\ell})}\). It proves equivalence to a max–min definition \(w_e=\max_{S
i e}\min_{T: f_T(\{e\})
eq 0} rac{x(S\setminus T)}{f_T(S)}=\min_T\max_{S
i e} rac{x(S\setminus T)}{f_T(S)}\) (Theorem 3.2), and establishes structural properties: monotonicity/continuity, feasibility indication \(w\le 1\iff x(S)\le f(S)\  orall S\), locality, and duality \(L_f(w)=\sum_e x_e\) (Props. 3.4–3.7).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a dynamic algorithm to maintain the density-based decomposition of a matroid restriction \(V'\) under single-element insertions/deletions, more efficiently than recomputing via submodular minimization. This paper is not about dynamic updates, but it directly studies essentially the same densest-set contraction/principal-partition-type decomposition (Algorithm 1) for a polymatroid defined by a submodular function \(f\), and relates it to principal partition sequences of matroids. Thus it provides a clean characterization of the decomposition and strong structural properties (monotonicity, locality, Lipschitz under small-bids) that could be leveraged to design incremental update rules. However, it does not present data structures or update-time guarantees for maintaining the decomposition under element insertions/deletions in \(V'\) (or changes in \(x\)/ground set), so it does not resolve the dynamic maintenance problem.

sources.title: Sparsification for graph and matroid optimization problems
sources.theorem_or_result: The thesis develops and analyzes a greedy density-based decomposition of a matroid (Chapter 5.2) and uses it to construct a sparsifier for matroid intersection called a Density-Constrained Subset (DCS). In particular, it proves existence/constructibility of a DCS of size \(O(|S_{opt}|/\varepsilon)\) that contains a \((3/2+\varepsilon)\)-approximate maximum common independent set for matroid intersection (Theorem 5.2, as summarized in the thesis abstract/intro), with applications to one-way communication (Theorem 5.5) and random-order streaming (Theorem 5.6).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an efficient dynamic (single-element insertion/deletion) maintenance algorithm for the density-based decomposition \(U_1,\dots,U_k\) of a changing subset \(V'\subseteq V\) in a fixed matroid \(M\), improving over recomputation via submodular minimization. This thesis uses essentially the same density notion \(\rho_M(U)=|U|/\operatorname{rank}_M(U)\) and explicitly introduces/uses a greedy “density-based decomposition” (Chapter 5.2) as a structural tool to build sparsifiers for matroid intersection. However, the provided excerpted material does not present any algorithm for dynamically updating the decomposition under insertions/deletions of elements of \(V'\); the algorithms discussed are for streaming/random-order and for constructing sparsifiers (DCS/DBS), not for fully dynamic maintenance of the decomposition itself. Thus it contributes relevant structural understanding and motivation but does not resolve the dynamic-update problem.

sources.title: Online Matroid Intersection: Submodular Water-Filling and Matroidal Welfare Maximization
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper defines a “water level” vector for poly-matroids (monotone submodular functions) via an iterative densest-set-and-contract procedure (Algorithm 1), equivalent to level sets given by submodular minimization 
\[S_f^*(y)=\text{unique maximal minimizer of }\min_{S\subseteq E}(f(S)-y(S))\]
and to an SUA-market convex program; it proves key properties (monotonicity, feasibility indication, duality, locality) and uses them to obtain a deterministic polynomial-time \((1-1/e)\)-competitive algorithm for fractional online poly-matroid intersection (Theorem 1.1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a dynamic (single-element insert/delete) algorithm to maintain the density-based decomposition of a matroid restriction, where each step selects a maximum-cardinality densest set and contracts it. This paper is not about dynamic updates, but it develops essentially the same kind of densest-set contraction chain (principal partition / density-based decomposition) as a central object: Algorithm 1 repeatedly finds a (unique maximal) densest set with respect to a weight vector \(x\) and contracts it, producing nested sets/levels. It also characterizes level sets via submodular minimization \(\min_S\{t\,f(S)-x(S)\}\), which is the same primitive used in naive recomputation in the open problem. These structural characterizations and locality/monotonicity properties describe how the decomposition changes as \(x\) changes, and could plausibly be leveraged toward efficient incremental maintenance under updates, but the paper does not provide an algorithm or complexity bounds for maintaining the decomposition under insertions/deletions of elements of \(V'\).
